speaker: interviewer
text: Of an interview with Mr. Semyon Menyuk.

speaker: interviewee
text: What the terrible time what I explain you what I got. It's not an easy time. You cannot tell everything for so many years. It's past, it's ago. Nor what has happened with me, I will never forgot that how long I will live. In this, not only I can tell the stories for this time but we in America, I got a lot of letters from Ukraine people what they write about me about that time, but terrible, not only about a terrible time what worse cannot be. What I can explain or translate a little from one letter what I got from the city what I was in ghetto, that's Kolki, city Kolki In it, it says, that-- I will translate this shortly. It's in the Russian language. It can tell a lot more. What they write, 'People remember this place where it was killed a lot of Jewish people and how it was the people what they see with this how it's hard, the time to watch for this, not only to be killed. How they take off the clothes. How they break the teeth from their mouth and take off the silver, the gold teeth from the mouth. The noise and the blood what it was, in the ground where they killed. It was like in life, it comes up and down. And how murderers, the Germans and the Ukraine Polizei, the police, what was looking on this. How they can kill the people like children, like women. One witness how one young boy, he didn't like this, and he's with a lot of energy. And he was trying to fighting with the Germans and the police. And he was jumping, he jumping from the truck and begin to run away. The police and the Germans was shouting after him, and he come out and down and run away from them. He was crying and noising. No, he was running. And he came-- no, the father and the mother in his family was killed in this time, and he lived by his self. And his name is Semyon Menyuk. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And how it was hard because the Germans told that nobody will give food to the Jewish when they jumped from the ghettos and want to stay anywhere by Ukraine. Now he comes to his Komarow, his village, where he knows and people knows him. And he was fighting all the years. And after the war he came to our city, Kolki, and he was working in the system, government's business. And his parents and his family is in the ground here for 49 years, and it stayed the big trees here where the beginning was playing here children and big adult people. Now is nothing. Nobody plays. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we wish him, Semyon, him and his family all the best, life what it can be. And take from us this [NON-ENGLISH] for a present and never forgot the city Kolki.' I got more, a lot of letters. When they send me letters but they said how the police came and was looking for me to kill me. They said but nobody was save me, and they wish me all the best in of my life. And I think it will be a time-- maybe, I don't know when-- I want to see this and to say to good people what they helped me in my life to be in life what they helped me a lot, to tell them thank you for everything what is done. And then the end, I want to say, it's nice to hear now, to have the museums everywhere in cities that's in Washington, in Israel, Yad Vashem, and I think in Los Angeles. And the people was working. It's un-profit organization, and now it's not long time ago, it's come Shoah Foundation. That's made the foundation Mr. Steven, uh, Mr. Spielberg. I want to tell to them, to all of them, to the old people to thank them for that job what they're doing but not paying. They're not receiving nothing for this. And to remember from generation to generation, it will be to remind the people what we got in our time. It's left not too much witness what can explain for that time because it was other people, a lot of people gone, not now with us. And when the 6 million people, when they in life and they come, it will be stories, a lot of stories. Without them now must be only we need to tell for them the stories. And thank you very much for all the people, the same what to our example for today what I am giving the interview for the people. I appreciate very, very much, and I wish everybody and to you the best. Be healthy and to have in life the best. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you Mr. Semyon. We appreciate the wonderful interview you've given us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're rolling. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture what you see now, it's made approximately 1932 in village Komarow, and it was a wedding by my cousin, [NON-ENGLISH] Menyuk and [NON-ENGLISH]. That's what you see in the center. Here is I am, myself, my sister, my mother, my father, and all the family relatives. That's what you see. That's me, my sister, and my mother and father and all relatives from the wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: There the picture what you see, that's my father and mother sitting. The name, the father's name is Moshe and the mother's name is Moshke, and the lady what she's standing, that's my cousin from Sarny, Freidel Perlmutter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The year is approximately 1920, '21.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's the picture what you see, there's the family, my mother's sister. What you see there, the older lady what in the middle, it's the name my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Aunt.

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt, [NON-ENGLISH] Perlmutter. And near to her, that's the man with the beard on the right side, there's the uncle, that's Abel Perlmutter. And near to him, the young lady on the right side, that's their daughter [NON-ENGLISH] Perlmutter. And near the end on the left side, that's a son [NON-ENGLISH] Perlmutter. And the picture's approximately in 1935. And the child, what it's from, I don't remember who is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there's the picture what you see, that's my wife's relatives. And the picture is done about 1927, '28. And now there was survived after the Holocaust time, and they stay now in Korosten, that's Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what you're seeing, that's my cousin. And this was after this they were married. And you saw before where there's the people. And her name is [NON-ENGLISH] Menyuk, and his name is [NON-ENGLISH]. And the picture is approximately done by 1936, '35, '36. And this picture give to me after the war, a priest from our village Komarow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where it was taken? Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture is taken in Kolki, in Kolk.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is done in 1957, approximately, and it's in Kolki, after the war, when we came to Kolki. And that's me, myself. And the name you know, that's Semyon Menyuk And that's my wife, Manya Menyuk, and her maiden name is Lerman, Manya.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This, a picture, it's made by 1948. And it's in the right side, that's my wife, Manya. On the left side, it's my wife's sister, Rania and the child is our son, Misha. And that's me in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say where it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture's done in Kolki.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a city picture. That's after the war when I came to Kolki. I went to this place where from the same place what I jumped from the truck, and I was running up. You see where this on the right side, and that's a top place where it was they killed all the Jewish people. And the place what I'm standing is where I jumped from the truck. And it's done by 1947 or '48 in Kolki.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: What this picture what you see, that's all the place where that's in the right side and left side and up, this was graves where the place where they were shooting and put from the ghetto and killed the people. That's approximately about 7,000 people. And now you see I'm standing in the grave. And I opened the grave, and it was-- I take some skeletons up and there was looking in near to me, that's four Jewish men. One is a doctor, what he was. The second is, the other is a neighbor in Kolki when we come. And in the left side is the guy that's our chauffeur. And I came, and with this I opened the grave. And it was one what they put, the Germans put the lime to fester, to melt the bodies what they killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: He'll make it larger. You can't see it right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what you see. That's a monument. It's done in Kolk in the place where it was killed the Jewish people from the ghetto. And it's come to visit and stand to open the monument. It's come from Israel. It's came people from America. They said, well, we have the letter. That's the left side, the first lady, but she read a poem about me and was looking for me to come to the opening, the [NON-ENGLISH]. And it says on the monument, the monument what was in this place was killed in 1942 year, is killed the Jewish population from Kolki. And it says it was killed innocent people. The murderers, the German murderers and together with the Ukraine murderers. The monument, it was done by 1992, and I am very appreciative for the Ukraine people what so many years when it was the Russian system, it was nothing. And now the people from Kolki, Ukraine people most, now them have at all Jewish people in this city. And they write me letters to-- they invited me to come to see. And what that's the lady what you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Left side, that's the lady what she wrote a poem about me. The name is [NON-ENGLISH] and she has done a lot of things to make the monument done. And now she is looking for the place to help them clean. She write me a letter, but she's working now. She's main bookkeeper in hospital, and she's looking, she send to me some flowers from this place to remember this place in ever for God. And other people. She's written about other people. It's always very clean, this place. And she told me in the letter that you see what these people in yarmulkes, that's they came from Israel. She don't know the names, and she don't know the addresses of them. And most of them, they came, they survived from the time, some one's only what they came to see the place from where they come. That's it. That's what you see the picture. That's my wife's parents. You see the father, Aron Lerman and on the right side is the mother, Bronia Lerman. And the picture is done in 1957 in Kiev. This you see the picture is done in 1957. That's in Kiev. That's my family. That's I'm sitting, Semyon Menyuk, and that to we are two sitting, my wife, Manya Menyuk. And that on left side in the red short dress, that's our daughter-in-law, [NON-ENGLISH] Menyuk. And then next to her, that's our daughter, Faina Menyuk. And that's on the right side near to her, that's Jacob Menyuk our younger son. And in the top is our older son, Misha Menyuk. And the picture's done before we left Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: To America, 1957.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Kiev.

speaker: interviewer
text: 14 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: That you see the picture is our family. The picture is done in 1995 in New York. That's sitting, my wife is seating left of me, Manya Menyuk. I'm sitting, Semyon Menyuk. And then from the left, the boy, that's our grandson. Igor Menyuk, and the other one to him, what he hold the hand on me, that's Oleg Menyuk. And the third one, our grandson in the glasses is Aaron Najmi. In right side, that's his father, Marvin Najmi. And that's our daughter near to him, that's the mother, Faina Menyuk Najmi. Near to her, the youngest son, Jacob Menyuk And near to her, that's the daughter-in-law, Victoria Menyuk. And near to her, the older son, Michael Menyuk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's, you see, the picture, that's always we have grandsons, three grandsons. That's the first girl. She was born the 24th day of February. She was 10 months old, and the picture is done in New York. And she is with the parents and her name is Firozah Rachelle Najmi. This means, Firozah, the first name is her father's mother's name, and Rachelle is my sister's name. And Najmi, that's after the father's last name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This what you see. That's in 1982, I was invited to Baltimore to opening a monument for the 6 million Jews what was killed in the time, Holocaust. And it was in Baltimore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mention the story inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the story about me is inside. And the story calls, 'A Second Time Through Hell.' [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] That's what you see, the book, the name in Yiddish it was, he write the book, his name is Daniel [NON-ENGLISH] on Yiddish language and the name of the book is [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means from-- from second time from the hell. The book is written in Warsaw in 1983. And it says, it write everything about Kolki ghetto. A lot of people, how it was everything. And separate about my life in Kolk in the time from the ghetto and all about me. How bad it was with me, how I ran from the ghetto, from the killing, and where I live, everything. It's, I think, the book, it's only not a lot of these books. I never saw more. It's only what this book above Kolk, Kolki.
